context.description: A reflective prose passage on awareness, breathing, and the limits of conscious control. It uses the image of a fence around perception and a jungle analogy to explain how what leaves awareness seems to vanish from our world. The page argues that breathing is a useful example because it repeatedly moves in and out of awareness.
normalization_notes: German prose page about consciousness and awareness; no chapter metadata visible.
Title inferred only from source file name not promoted as a field.

rows.text_content: Breathing is repeatedly noticed as something one can consciously control, illustrating how awareness and self-control are routinely overlooked.
rows.paragraph_text: Once something enters awareness, it belongs to the circle of one's world and can be observed, named, remembered, and sometimes controlled.

rows.text_content: What falls outside awareness disappears from perception and seems to stop existing for consciousness, even when it could still be influenced.
rows.paragraph_text: The text uses breathing and the inability to know whether one unconsciously reads every license plate as examples of this limit.

rows.text_content: A scientist exploring a magical jungle serves as an analogy for how awareness can shrink the perceived size of the world until it seems to match only what the eyes can reach.
rows.paragraph_text: The passage prepares a deeper explanation of the boundary of awareness by shifting from breath to a larger metaphor.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness German nonfiction philosophy page on consciousness, awareness, breathing, and the boundary of perception. Breathing is used as a recurring example, followed by a fence metaphor and a jungle analogy to explain how awareness defines what feels real and knowable.
